data.title: Cracker Barrel: Sharp Traffic Declines And Severe Margin Erosion
data.description:
2026 has been an especially volatile year in the stock markets, but at the same time, for consumer-facing businesses, much of the retrenchment in share prices is very much driven by fundamentals. Restaurants, in particular, are having trouble convincing diners to eat out amid sharp menu price inflation, and most restaurant chains are reporting negative comp sales.

Cracker Barrel (CBRL), the diner-style chain that sparked furor last year over a scrapped logo change, is no exception. Over the past year, shares of Cracker Barrel have lost ~20% of their value as sales have contracted. That said, the stock is up modestly since January, an outlier among restaurant chains, as the company's recent earnings print included a modest boost to guidance. The question for investors now is, has Cracker Barrel bottomed, or is this just a dead-cat bounce?

I last wrote a sell article on Cracker Barrel in December, when the stock was trading closer to $27 per share. Since then, Cracker Barrel has actually outperformed the crash in the rest of the stock market, though to be fair, the stock had also been deeply pummeled in 2025 (losing two-thirds of its value after the logo change scandal). With no evidence that fundamentals have any chance of a turnaround, I'm reiterating my sell rating on the stock - in my view, Cracker Barrel's brief outperformance in 2026 isn't likely to last long.

To me, these are the core red flags that investors should be wary about in this stock:

  • Persistently weak traffic trends. Cracker Barrel is suffering ~10% declines in traffic. Right now, its comp sales trends are being held up by higher menu pricing (which is a ~4 point bump to comp sales). As the company laps these menu price increases in calendar 2026, we expect comp sales to deteriorate further.
  • Tarnished brand image. Cracker Barrel suffered a massive blowback in public opinion when the company attempted and then reversed its decision to change its logo in 2025 (even drawing attention from President Trump). Since then, consistently worsening sales trends suggest that the company has alienated a large chunk of its customer base.
  • Entering into a highly promotional restaurant environment. Competitors, especially in the fast-food arena, are leaning more into value meals in order to encourage dining out at a time when consumers feel pinched. Cracker Barrel has also responded by releasing its own value meals and stepping up its promotions. In the long haul, this will have the impact of offsetting the company's price increases and will weaken its margins.
  • Commodity and labor inflation are eating into adjusted EBITDA. It's not just the need to offer deeper promotions that are a major risk to margins. The company is also expecting low-/mid-single-digit inflation in raw commodities and hourly wage labor, which is positioning the stock to decline to a low-single-digit adjusted EBITDA margin - a low even among restaurant peers.
  • Strained balance sheet. The company has very little cash remaining and over $0.5 billion in debt, which sits at a >5x leverage ratio against the company's expected adjusted EBITDA.

In my view, this is a company with a very limited path back into the market's good graces. Its only potential upside is a relatively cheap valuation. At current share prices near $29, Cracker Barrel trades at a market cap of $645.7 million. Netting off the $8.6 million of cash and $531.4 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $1.17 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Cracker Barrel ending in July 2026), the company has slightly upped its guidance to $3.24-$3.27 billion in sales, which is a tighter range but with a slightly higher $3.255 billion midpoint that implies a -6.5% y/y sales decline.

The bigger updates to the company's outlook are slightly lower than expected inflation, with commodity inflation now expected at 2.0-2.5% (75bps lower at the midpoint) and wage inflation now expected at 2.5-3.0% (also 75bps lower at the midpoint). This has resulted in a boost in the company's adjusted EBITDA range of $85-$100 million, or a 2.8% margin at the midpoint and slightly higher than the midpoint of the previously wider $70-$110 million range.

This positions Cracker Barrel's valuation at 12.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. This is by no means expensive, but when we consider that other struggling restaurant chains like Wendy's (WEN) and Restaurant Brands International (QSR), the parent of Burger King, are trading at in-line or lower multiples, we find Cracker Barrel entirely not compelling.

Continue to steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q2 Recap

Let's now go through Cracker Barrel's fiscal Q2 (January quarter) results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Cracker Barrel's revenue declined -8% y/y to $874.8 million, nevertheless slightly outperforming Wall Street's expectations of $864.3 million (-9% y/y) by a one-point margin.

The big issue here remains the company's comp sales trends, which, as a reminder, measure the revenue growth from continuously open stores and exclude the contribution of newly opened or recently closed locations. Comp restaurant sales in Q2 declined -7.1%, which worsened relative to a -4.7% decline in Q1 (and was substantially worse relative to 5.4% growth in Q4).

Again, as previously mentioned, higher menu pricing helped to blunt the impact of a ~10% decline in traffic. Management optimistically noted that traffic trends improved throughout the quarter and declined "only" -9% in January; still, we have little confidence amid a shaky macro that Cracker Barrel can truly recover to positive traffic.

Speaking to detailed traffic trends on the Q2 earnings call, CFO Craig Pommells noted as follows:

Comparable store restaurant sales decreased by 7.1%, which included a traffic decline of 10.1%. From a monthly perspective, November and December traffic both declined between 10% and 11%. We were encouraged by the improvement in January, which declined 9%, including an approximately 50 basis point net year-over-year unfavorable impact from weather.

Restaurant average check increased 3.4% and included pricing of 4.2%. Menu mix was negative, driven primarily by higher discounts. Off-premise sales were 23.6% of restaurant sales, which increased modestly over prior year. Total retail revenue decreased 9.3% to $180.5 million, and comparable store retail sales decreased by 9.2%."

Core to the company's strategy to pull customers back into Cracker Barrel stores is a heightened focus on price. In the fall, the company launched a 2-for-1 meal deal priced at $19.99, available on weekdays and offering two main courses alongside sides or desserts. The company is also trying to drive upsell via low-priced add-ons, including extra sides and $5 for a soup and salad add-on.

Still, we note that the cost burden of promotions, on top of diseconomies of scale from a shrinking comp sales base, eroded the company's margins. Adjusted EBITDA in Q2 clocked in at $38.2 million, down -49% y/y.

Adjusted EBITDA margins of 4.4% also eroded -350bps y/y versus 7.9% margins in the year-ago Q2. For a company shouldering a debt burden at a 5.7x leverage ratio ($522.8 million in debt net of cash, divided by $92.5 million in midpoint expected EBITDA this year), the sharp deterioration in margins bears additional scrutiny.

Key Takeaways

With decelerating comp sales, sharply declining traffic, eroding margins, and a huge debt load, there's little to like about Cracker Barrel. Yes, it's true that the company slightly lifted its (already downcast) outlook for FY26, but I fail to see any appeal in this stock at ~13x adjusted EBITDA when the company's EBITDA profile is in a sharp y/y decline. Avoid this stock and invest elsewhere.

data.title: XPEL: Why This Stock Could Deliver Strong Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

At first glance, XPEL Inc. (XPEL) looks expensive and trades at a premium compared to peers. But in my view, that premium is justified as the fundamentals of the underlying business continue to improve. Based on my analysis, the revenue has the potential to grow, driven by the acquisition of China assets, EBITDA margin recovery, strong FCF generation, a unique business model with the DAP platform and a company-owned installation center, and a growing addressable market. Furthermore, it is trading at almost half its own 5-year historical EV/EBITDA average. As margins recover to historical levels and increase in revenue from China operations, there is a reasonable case for the valuation multiple to move higher from current levels.

The company is a global provider of protective films and coatings for automotive protection. Its products include paint protection films, window film, ceramic coating, and windshield protection film. In terms of channel strategy, it sells products through aftermarket, dealership, OEM, and e-commerce.

China Distribution Acquisition: Has Potential to Accelerate Revenue Growth

Before the distribution acquisition in China, the operations were managed through a 3rd-party distribution channel partnership. From a business perspective, this was not adding full value to the operations, as XPEL had limited control over pricing decisions, inventory management, or channel development. Through this acquisition, the company can run operations independently now. This is a big stock catalyst, as XPEL now has access to the large automotive market. According to the CAAM analysis of 2025, China maintained the top global position in automobile production, and sales reached 34.5 million and 34.4 million units, respectively.

To understand the significance of this deal, we have to evaluate the latest quarterly results, which are the first full quarter post-acquisition of China distribution assets. The contribution from China significantly increased by 65.3% to $39.9 million, and its share of total revenue increased from 5.7% in FY 2024 to 8.4% in FY 2025. The change in contribution to total revenue is significant given its revenue from just a single quarter, and on a full-year basis, this will be higher in the future. With direct operational control, the growth opportunity from China is significantly larger than what the investors expected from this deal. Although the stock is trading at a premium to peers, given this structural change in channel strategy, I believe the stock can further outperform and deserves a higher valuation.

In a nutshell, the investor should track this revenue growth, and, given the size of the China market, the stock has a potential for further substantial upside.

Paint Protection Film Market: Competitive Strength Within a Large Addressable Market

XPEL competes in the paint protection film market against 3M (MMM), Eastman Chemical Company (EMN), Avery Dennison (AVY), and Saint-Gobain (CODYY). All these are diversified companies with much broader product offerings compared to XPEL. However, XPEL is a purely focused player with leaner operations to design products as per the requirements of the industry.

The DAP (Design Access Platform) software platform from XPEL is the key differentiating unique factor that sets it apart from all competitors. This platform is used by 90,000 certified installers who pay monthly access fees to design and cut paint protection film to perfectly fit as per the specific vehicle models. Once installers are trained on the DAP platform and include it in their operations, it's not easy to switch to a competitor. It's the same if we are using Microsoft Suite for workflow; then moving to another platform is not easy and involves switching costs too. Further, the company also operates company-owned installation centers and has a dedicated dealership service, which is different compared to other players. In FY 2025, service revenue consists of 24% of total revenue, which includes DAP platform fees, installation labor from company-owned installation centers, and dealership services. In a nutshell, this recurring revenue base is a unique advantage over competitors who sell only products. For investors, it's important to understand this unique proposition by having a business model consisting of product and service offerings. Such business models should deserve a premium valuation too.

According to Grand View Research, the global paint protection film market was estimated at $502.55 million in 2024 and is estimated to reach $726.63 million by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 6.6%. Within Asia-Pacific, China will be the largest market where XPEL has direct operations now. Other predictions from Mordor Intelligence and Research and Markets suggest growth of 5.41% and 7.16%, respectively. Based on all three estimates, we can conclude that XPEL is operating in the industry with positive growth, as the market is growing by 5–7% annually over the next 5 years. The revenue growth of XPEL is 13.3% in FY 2025, which indicates strong performance by generating double the industry average growth rate. I believe with the inclusion of China, the revenue growth will further accelerate in the next few years.

In a nutshell, XPEL has competitive strength through its DAP platform and company-owned installation centers and operates within a large addressable market expected to grow by 5–7% annually over the next 5 years.

EBITDA Margin on Path of Recovery

The EBITDA margin analysis is important for understanding the margin recovery, as annual and quarterly margins signal different trends. By analyzing the quarterly data, we understand the recovery in the underlying business with improvement in margin.

Data in $ million FY 2023 FY 2024 FY 2025 Q4 2024 Q4 2025
Revenue 396.3 420.4 472.2 107.5 122.3
Growth 6.1% 12.3% 13.8%
Gross Margin 42.2% 42.2% 40.6% 41.9%
EBITDA 76.9 69.5 77.4 14.3 19.6
Margin 19.4% 16.5% 16.4% 13.3% 16.0%

Data calculated by the author from FY 2025 10-K and Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation

In FY 2025, the gross margin was at 42.2%, which is similar to FY 2024. However, the EBITDA margin decreased slightly from 16.5% to 16.3% due to operating expenses increasing by 17.1% in FY 2025. The higher expenses were driven primarily by expenses associated with the China acquisition. In other words, the margin compression was the result of one-off expenses due to M&A and not a deterioration. On a quarterly basis, the situation is more encouraging for the investors. In Q4 2025, the EBITDA margin was 16.0%, compared to 13.3% in Q4 2024, up by 37.6% year-over-year. What's the reason for this sudden improvement? The drag in 2024 was due to inventory issues with the OEM, an escalation in operating expenses to support international expansion, and dealership. By Q4 2025, all three factors had been improved, resulting in margin recovery. For investors, it's important to understand that the company is operationally strong and withstands the industry cycle. To substantiate this, management has guided that gross margins are expected to improve in Q2 2026.

For comparison, the sector operates at median EBITDA margins of 11.01%. Whereas, XPEL already has margins above sector level and has a clear path to recovery toward a 5-year average margin of the 17 to 18% range. This is an important indicator for investors because the cost structure was increased by a temporary factor, not a structural issue with the business.

Strong Cash Generation Confirms Earnings Quality

Data in $ million FY 2023 FY 2024 FY 2025
Cash From Operations 37.4 47.8 66.9
Growth 27.8% 40.0%
Capex 6.3 6.7 4.0
Free Cash Flow (CFO-Capex) 31.1 41.1 62.9
FCF Growth 32.2% 53.0%
EBITDA 76.9 69.5 77.4
Cash From Operations as % of EBITDA 48.6% 68.8% 86.4%

Data calculated by the author from FY 2025 10-K and Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation

Before we move to the latest financials, let me share an important historical trend. The company generated the highest cash flow in FY 2025. Further, in the last 10 years, all years had positive cash flows. For a company that is operating in a cyclical automotive industry, this is exceptional performance and resilience of the business model.

In FY 2025, the cash from operations reached $66.9 million, an increase of 40% from $47.8 million in FY 2024. This represents a very high conversion ratio of 86%. For comparison, the conversion ratio was 69% in FY 2024 and 49% in FY 2023. Why is this conversion ratio important for investors to track? The continuous improvement in cash conversion is significant because it shows profits translating into actual cash due to business efficiency.

Further, FCF increased by 53% to $62.9 million in FY 2025. The balance sheet is clean with negligible debt and gives management significant decision-making options for business operations. In this case, with $62.9 million in FCF and no meaningful debt, the company has the flexibility to invest in channel strategy, pursue inorganic growth, and return capital to shareholders. According to management, further strategic investments in manufacturing and the supply chain are planned for 2026. Based on this, we can conclude that the balance sheet provides strong support for both investment and shareholder returns as revenue accelerates. For investors, this is a unique proposition to pick a stock that has strong fundamentals and is almost debt-free.

Valuation

Peers EV/EBITDA (Forward)
XPEL, Inc. (XPEL) 13.31x
Dorman Products, Inc. (DORM) 9.06x
PHINIA Inc. (PHIN) 6.34x
BorgWarner Inc. (BWA) 6.02x
Genuine Parts Company (GPC) 9.85x
Peers Average 8.92x
Sector Median 9.44x
XPEL 5-Year Average 24.44x

Sourced from Seeking Alpha

XPEL is currently trading at a 49% premium to the peer average of 8.92x based on the forward EV/EBITDA multiple. Although this looks expensive at first glance, a detailed fundamental analysis explains a different and much more positive investment thesis. The company is growing at double-digit revenue growth with a software-based unique model, a direct distribution channel strategy, and significant international expansion. With such a situation, I believe stocks trading at a premium are justified.

For investors, the more interesting fact is the stock is currently trading at almost half its historical average EV/EBITDA multiple. As margins recover to historical levels and increase in revenue from China operations, there is a reasonable case for the valuation multiple to move higher from current levels.

However, investors have to closely watch the recovery in margin, integration of China operations, and automobile demand. As a delay in margin recovery or integration issue in China could be a negative catalyst for the stock.

Final Thoughts

XPEL is a strong company growing revenue at double the industry growth rate over the next 5 years, recovering margin with movement to higher historical margin, generating strong free cash flow, and crucially integrating the China story. I suggest waiting for Q1 2026 results to confirm the margin recovery direction and more data on China operations before initiating a position. For existing investors, this is a long-term investment thesis and reasonable to stick with investment with the caveat of broader automobile sector demand.

data.title: AngioDynamics Growing, But With More Noise Than The Street Likes
data.description:
AngioDynamics (ANGO) has been looking more and more like a “two steps forward, one and a half steps back” company. Management has inarguably made progress in building the company’s growth-oriented Medical Tech business and boosted the company’s overall growth profile, but quarter-to-quarter performance has remained erratic and unpredictable, and progress in margin expansion has likewise been unsteady.

AngioDynamics shares are down about 20% since my last update, with two sizable selloffs after the last two quarterly earnings reports tied to growth, margins/reinvestment, and perhaps the pending retirement of the CEO.

At this point I do think AngioDynamics shares are likely undervalued, but this is not a stock for investors who get anxious about short-term market reactions. The Med Tech business does appear to be on solid footing, with sizable addressable markets ahead, but there’s still a lot of work to do before these shares will reliably sustain a better multiple.

Another Quarter of “Mixed” Trends

AngioDynamics’ third quarter offered food for both the bulls and bears, with the latter ultimately winning out on weaker guidance for fourth quarter EBITDA.

Revenue rose 9%, beating by about 2%, with the Med Tech business growing 19% (following 13% growth in FQ2, 26% growth in FQ1, and 22% growth in FQ4’25) and the Medical Devices business growing about 1%.

AlphaVac continues to perform well off of a small base, logging 47% growth on increased usage in pulmonary embolisms. AngioVac saw 5% growth but a small sequential decline. Auryon rose 18%, which is strong against an underlying market growing in the low double-digits, but I’d frankly been hoping that this business would be consistently growing at 20%+ by now. NanoKnife revenue rose 21%, with probe revenue up 20% (and about 4% sequentially), as usage grows in the wake of FDA approval for prostate cancer and a new CPT code that simplifies reimbursement.

Gross margin took a meaningful step back this quarter, with overall gross margin declining 110bp yoy and 350bp qoq largely due to $1.3M in new tariff costs. Med Tech gross margin was stable yoy at 62.5% but down 280bp qoq, while Med Device margin was 44.2% vs. 47.4% a year ago and 49.1% in the prior quarter.

Adjusted EBITDA improved 38% year over year, though the margin remains around 2% (well below most “quality growth” med-techs).

Weak Guidance for Q4, but Not Necessarily for Bad Reasons

All told, revenue performance was pretty good. I’m encouraged by the strong uptake in AlphaVac, though slightly disappointed in Auryon (which has more to do with my expectations/hopes than true underperformance). NanoKnife is a tougher call. It seems churlish to be disappointed with 20%+ growth, but I would like to see more momentum in probes.

On the flip side, there’s work to do on margins. The tariff situation is an “is what it is” situation, particularly as a company of AngioDynamics’ size doesn’t have a lot of mitigation options in the short term. Likewise, there’s just not enough scale in the business yet to fully offset needed reinvestment in SG&A and R&D, though I think more could likely be done to improve (or sell) the remaining Medical Device businesses.

Management’s guidance for the fourth quarter didn’t go over well with the market. While revenue guidance was raised again (and is about 2% higher than the initial guidance for FY’26), and gross margins were guided stable, EBITDA guidance implies a pretty weak fourth quarter.

To that end, management did confirm increased spending, largely on the clinical side. Likewise, management indicated that they’d be using cash to build inventory ahead of some pre-announced business interruptions at the company’s sterilization service provider.

Wall Street is a “what have you done for me lately?” sort of place, but I don’t see this guidance as particularly egregious. Management has the upcoming APEX-RETURN and PAVE studies to fund, both of which could expand the use (and revenue) of AlphaVac and AngioVac, respectively, and there is an ongoing study for Auryon in below-the-knee peripheral atherectomy (AMBITION BTK). I regard these as “takes money to make money” propositions, and arguably the Street should have been expecting an uptick in R&D spending.

As for the inventory build, what is the alternative? It’s impractical to switch sterilization providers on short notice, and it makes sense to me to build inventory ahead of a known disruption as a means of avoiding running out of stock and alienating customers (and/or forcing them to switch to competing products).

The Outlook

My expectations were lower than the Street’s, so not much of note happens with my model at this point. My revenue expectations are a bit higher now, but I’m still looking for around 6% annualized revenue growth over the next five years and long-term growth of over 5%.

Frankly, as the Med Tech business gains scale, I won’t be surprised to see AngioDynamics exit more of its legacy businesses. Between minimal revenue growth and weaker gross margins, there aren’t a lot of arguments for keeping them once the Med Tech business has scaled up.

As far as margins go, this remains a work in progress, particularly given the likelihood (in my view, at least) that management will seek to launch additional studies to expand the labels and marketing appeal of its core growth businesses (AlphaVac, Auryon, and NanoKnife). I think a mid-to-high single-digit EBITDA margin is still possible in FY’27, and I do think a double-digit margin within three years is likewise possible.

Valuation remains challenging, due in large part to the fact that the company isn’t growing fast enough for the Street to ignore the margin situation. With that, the Medical Device business effectively drags down the Med Tech business; I can argue for a $16-$17/share fair value based on a 2x multiple that blends a roughly 4x multiple for the Med Tech business with a sub-1x multiple for Medical Devices.

An upcoming CEO transition is also relevant to a discussion of AngioDynamics’ outlook. CEO Jim Clemmer, who has served for around 10 years now, has announced his intention to retire in FY’27, and the company is now searching for a replacement.

AngioDynamics shares are down about 20% over Clemmer’s tenure, but I would argue the business is on better footing now with the sale of multiple legacy businesses (where the company didn’t have bright growth/margin prospects) and with the repositioning around AngioVac/AlphaVac, Auryon, and NanoKnife. In any case, I’m curious to see who the board selects for the role, and I would expect that those growth businesses will remain the core of the business going forward.

The Bottom Line

My biggest issue with AngioDynamics is honestly the fact that there are a lot of investment options in the med-tech space, and many of them offer less messy, or at least less volatile, growth and value stories. That said, I do think that AngioDynamics is on the right track, and I do think the shares don’t fully reflect the potential of the growth business. For patient investors, I still see a worthwhile case for owning this stock, but that word “patient” is a key consideration here.

data.title: Oracle: OCI And Multicloud Execution Shift The Narrative To Buy (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my last coverage, which I rated as Sell, Oracle Corp. (ORCL) stock is down about 24% until 2 April 2026, as investors continue to have concerns about the company’s heavy asset-intensive business model regarding the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) business line and its high debt leverage.

ORCL remains with negative FCF, and its backlog depends on OpenAI; however, Q3’26 results showed that the company, on the one hand, reduced OpenAI’s dependency and, on the other hand, proved that the OCI business accelerates the overall growth trajectory, as well as being a key component for cross-selling opportunities leading to customer stickiness.

OCI Accelerates Oracle’s Growth Story via Revenue Acceleration but Also Through Business Restructuring

Oracle’s total revenue rose by 22% YoY to $17.2B in Q3’26, with the main driver being the Cloud segment, which grew by 44%. Specifically, Cloud growth came mainly from its AI infrastructure business, which grew by 84%, while applications grew a solid 13%.

As you can see, ORCL’s total revenue growth rate is strongly correlated with the Cloud segment, which shows a strong growth rate acceleration over the last two years due to high AI demand and, in my opinion, will continue to record higher rates in the following quarters, given the company’s guidance of 46%-50% in Q4’26.

The OCI story does not stop here. According to the CRN report, the OCI segment will have a 75% CAGR, broken down as follows:

  • Revenue of $34 in FY27
  • Revenue of $77B in FY28
  • Revenue of $129B in FY29
  • Revenue of $166B in FY30

Investors remain concerned due to Cloud growth coming with lower profitability, driven by the need for high capital investments mainly for GPUs, CPUs, and memory. Specifically, cloud operating profit of $8.7B in Q3’26 reflects an operating margin of 58%, which is slightly lower than the operating margin of 63% in Q3’25.

In contrast, Hardware and Service segments reported operating margins of 68% and 28% in Q3’26, respectively, compared to 64% and 20% in the previous year. Given that Cloud is the company’s main segment, overall segment-based operating margin reached 56% in Q3’26, down from 59% in Q3’25, but still at high levels.

The company’s overall operating margin increased to 32% in the last quarter, from almost 31% in the previous year, mainly due to cost efficiency improvements and reductions in R&D, administrative, and restructuring expenses.

That said, the company on March 31, 2026, announced layoffs of about 20,000-30,000 employees, which could lead to $8-$10B in free cash flow. I suggest investors should not be concerned about this because Oracle, like many other tech companies, replaced personnel in specific departments with AI tools.

Teams that were affected most are Revenue and Health Sciences (RHS) and SaaS and Virtual Operations Services (SVOS), with at least 30% job cuts per team. RHS is a team that develops software for the health industry, while the SVOS team manages SaaS solutions, such as ERP or HCM. In simple terms, Oracle fired developers and will use AI to produce code -a recently common cost-reduction strategy among many top-tier tech companies.

One more positive sign from the last quarter is the company’s backlog. Specifically, Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO) ended at $553B, up 325% YoY, related to large-scale AI contracts where the company does not expect to raise any additional funds to support these contracts. The key point, in my view, is that ORCL seems to gradually reduce its high concentration on OpenAI with new large companies.

Oracle Shifted Toward a Capital-Heavy IaaS Business Model, but Multicloud (Database) Solutions Could Reach Clients Outside Its Ecosystem

The company shifted its business model from a SaaS asset‑light business model towards a capital‑heavy IaaS one. This shift is reflected in high debt and CapEx, with a direct impact on FCF. Oracle continues to report negative FCF, given its high CapEx of $48B in Q3’26, and the company raised its expectation for the full year from $35B to $50B. I see that ORCL’s high revenue growth is leveraged with high debt -about $162B – due to massive investments, as I mentioned in my previous article.

But in the last quarter, the company stated something that changed my view:

Multicloud Database revenue grew 531% year-over-year. AI infrastructure revenue grew 243% year-over-year. Both also have demand that exceeds supply and a clear execution plan from Oracle that will rapidly turn that demand into profitable recurring revenue. We created our Multicloud partnerships with first Microsoft, then Google and finally, Amazon to bring the best database platform to all clouds… We have global region coverage in all of our partner clouds. We now have 33 regions that live with Microsoft and 14 live with Google. We delivered significant growth with AWS beginning Q3 with 2 AWS regions live, exiting Q3 with 8 AWS regions live, and we will exit Q4 with 22 AWS regions live… as I mentioned earlier, about our Multicloud Database business, that's a much higher margin business, more in the 60% to 80% range.

Let me first explain what Multicloud is based on Oracle's definition:

Multicloud is the coordinated use of cloud services from more than one cloud service provider. By using multiple providers, companies can avoid being tied down to one vendor and maintain their flexibility.

The company also highlights the benefits of using multicloud as follows:

Also, customers can utilize the best product from one cloud service provider and pair it with the best product from another cloud service provider, getting the best‑fit solution.

The key point here is that ORCL offers native cross-cloud solutions. Whereas Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) offer multicloud solutions by containerized workload mobility, ORCL co-allocates its own hardware -such as CPUs, GPUs, and storage servers - within other vendors' data centers.

Specifically, about Multicloud Database, it allows ORCL’s clients to run Oracle Exadata databases within Azure, Google Cloud, and AWS datacenters, eliminating egress charges, simplifying governance, and enabling high-speed, low-latency communication between OCI and other environments.

In my view, the most important benefit is that Oracle “wins” clients without the need for them to use its own cloud services; therefore, it gains revenue without allocating heavy investments in cloud or datacenters. As a result, the company clients it could not reach otherwise, as they do not use ORCL’s cloud solutions, delivering high margins in the range of 60%–80%. Based on the company, a multicloud win is Air France-KLM, which led to a 13x performance improvement with significantly lower cost for them.

High Concentration and Competition Are the Company’s Main Risks, While Valuation Seems Attractive After a Strong Price Dive

As I mentioned in my last article about Oracle, the company must deal with high concentration risk with OpenAI. It remains questionable how exactly OpenAI will find the capital to support its investments of about $300B in ORCL; as a result, ORCL’s future performance is under question too.

Competition is the other major risk concern. Despite ORCL cooperating with MSFT, GOOG, and AMZN by offering multicloud solutions, competition among them remains heavy, which puts pressure on profit margins.

PE and PEG metrics indicate that ORCL appears undervalued compared to hyperscalers but also compared to promising neo-cloud companies, such as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS). Notably, in terms of the PEG ratio, ORCL’s 0.97 value is strongly lower than its main peers.

Also, the company expects revenue of $67B in FY26 and raises total revenue guidance to $90B in FY27, reflecting YoY growth of almost 17% and 34%, respectively, making its FWD P/S ratio even more attractive compared to analyst guidance below.

Bottom Line

In conclusion, Oracle appears undervalued compared to my previous coverage in terms of valuation metrics, and its risk-reward profile seems more attractive to me after Q3’26 results. Investors should weigh up the fact that Oracle currently operates under an asset-heavy business model to offer IaaS solutions due to high AI demand and growth rate acceleration driven by these investments.

I noticed revenue growth rate acceleration over the last quarters, and I anticipate this rate to expand further. Overall, ORCL's risk-reward profile is attractive at current price levels, with asymmetric reward if the company expands its growth rate in the following quarters.

Based on all the above, I upgraded the stock from Sell to Buy, but I suggest investors hold a small position of ORCL in their portfolio, probably as a supportive high-risk, high-reward stock among other cloud stock companies.

data.title: Mid-America: A Dirt-Cheap Way To Buy Apartments
data.description:
Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) is an apartment REIT that owns class A assets in growing markets and that aren't encumbered with much debt. Excess supply and interest rate fears have caused the stock to fall, which is giving investors a nice opportunity to purchase the underlying apartments at an attractive discount to their true worth.

Introduction

Mid-America Apartments is a residential REIT with a portfolio of approximately 103,000 apartments spread mostly across the Sunbelt region of the United States. About three-quarters of the portfolio is located in large cities and their suburbs, while about a quarter of the units are located in mid-tier markets.

Large markets include metros like Atlanta, Dallas, Orlando, and Tampa Bay, while mid-tier markets include smaller cities like Charleston, Greenville, Fredericksburg, and Richmond.

The portfolio mostly consists of garden-style apartments, along with a good chunk of mid-rise units. Garden-style apartments have three stories or less, while mid-rise apartments are 4-9 stories. A high-rise is anything 10 stories or higher.

While the portfolio is diverse with exposure in many different markets, the REIT is fairly concentrated in its top five markets. More than 40% of net operating income (NOI) comes from these metro areas.

There are a few reasons why MAA has focused on the Sunbelt region of the United States. These areas are growing faster than most other areas of the country. Salaries are relatively high -- especially in cities -- and state income taxes are lower. That translates into more dollars in a tenant's pocket. Unemployment is low. The weather is nice. And the real estate itself is cheaper than most other parts of the country.

MAA specifically has an average lease-to-income level of around 20%, which is quite affordable.

Put it all together, and the Sunbelt offers a good deal for both the tenant and the landlord.

Renting is also compelling versus owning. Since the beginning of 2020, the average new lease price of an MAA apartment is 23% higher. Average rents went from $1,267 per month to $1,555. The estimated mortgage payment is 99% higher, increasing from $1,058 per month to $2,107. It's easy to see why tenants would choose to continue renting versus becoming homeowners.

I'll also mention the REIT's excellent balance sheet. Mid-America has a debt-to-assets ratio of 30.2%, which is one of the best balance sheets in the sector. Debt maturities are spread out as well, which reduces the risk of any interest rate shock upon debt refinancing. Most of the debt is fixed rate, too.

This is a company that is built to last, which is an attractive feature in today's uncertain markets.

And yet, despite all MAA has going for it, Mid-America shares have been weak over the last year, losing approximately 25% of their value. The stock is down almost 50% versus all-time highs set in the early part of 2022, too.

Investors are concerned about one main thing. Developers have been busy building for the last few years -- especially in the Sunbelt -- as they took advantage of low interest rates and an attractive outlook in the 2021-22 period. These apartments are now completed and competing with existing MAA units. This oversupply has temporarily pushed rents lower in a lot of different markets.

I believe this short-term weakness is a long-term investor's opportunity. Mid-America shares are cheap in a few different ways, especially when we break them down on a per-apartment basis.

Price Per Apartment

Let's do some quick math and figure out just how cheap Mid-America Properties is by breaking it down on a per-unit basis.

Then, we'll compare that number to the replacement value and see what kind of deal investors are getting today.

As of December 31st, 2005, Mid-American Properties had 102,814 completed apartment units in the portfolio. It also has a development program with further apartments in various stages of completion, but for the sake of this exercise, we'll ignore those properties. We'll also ignore MAA's 35% ownership in a Washington, D.C. property.

We'll use a very simple formula to figure out the value per unit. We'll add the company's market cap plus its debt (including preferred shares) to get the total value of the portfolio. After all, that's what an investor is paying for it today.

We'll then take that number and divide it by the number of units.

As I type this, MAA has a market cap of $14.7B. It owes $4.4B in long-term debt and $299.5M in short-term debt. This debt is offset by $60M in cash. This gives us an enterprise value of $19.34B.

(Note: all figures come from Seeking Alpha's balance sheet page)

Divide this by 102,814 units, and we get a value per suite of just over $188,000.

Now that we have a number, a bigger question emerges. Is this actually cheap?

I argue it is for a few different reasons. First, let's take a look at MAA's own development portfolio. The company has 3,245 units in various stages of completion in seven different markets. These are expected to cost it $1.15B to complete, for an average cost of $355,000 per unit. That's nearly twice as expensive on a per-unit basis as the implied value investors are getting from buying the stock.

We can also look at MAA's own recent acquisition history to see what management thinks is a fair price to pay on a per-unit basis. Over the last two years, Mid-America has acquired four different communities, spending $370.5M to acquire 1,320 units. That works out to $280,681 per unit, or about a 5.5% cap rate. That's about 50% higher than the implied value of MAA's current portfolio.

Finally, let's value the entire MAA portfolio just like a real estate investor would value a rental property or an apartment complex. We'll look at the cap rate.

In 2025, MAA generated $1.37B in NOI. As mentioned above, the portfolio is worth $19.34B. That gives us a cap rate of 7.1%. According to CBRE, typical cap rates in the Sunbelt part of the United States are between 4.5% and 5%. Investors are getting a nice deal by purchasing MAA shares versus the underlying real estate privately.

Whether we value MAA on a per-apartment-unit basis, a discount-to-replacement-value basis, or a cap rate basis, the conclusion is the same. The stock is cheap versus buying apartment units directly.

Traditional Valuation

Next, let's value MAA as we would any other stock -- on a multiple of funds from operations.

The REIT came out with 2026 guidance a few weeks ago that was admittedly a little weak. Management projected that FFO would be a little less than last year's number, as the company still sees some weakness in a few of its markets. FFO is expected to be $8.35 to $8.71 per share, for a midpoint of $8.53 per share.

Analysts tend to agree with that assessment. The Seeking Alpha earnings page says analysts expect $8.56 per share in FFO in 2026. That number is expected to increase to $8.73 per share in 2027.

That puts us at 14.6x 2026's FFO and 14.3x 2027's FFO. I believe that's cheap for a REIT that offers dependable earnings and has such a strong balance sheet.

Another factor we can analyze to see MAA's valuation is the dividend yield. This is a simple test that looks at how much dividend income the investment will generate versus the company's history. A high dividend yield often indicates the stock is undervalued, while a lower dividend yield can signal the opposite.

The evidence here is clear. Mid-America's shares are cheap on a dividend yield basis. The current yield is just over 5%, which is the company's highest payout in over a decade.

Despite the larger than normal dividend yield, investors can count on the payout. The payout ratio is around 70% of 2026's projected FFO, and the company has a 15-year dividend growth streak it will want to project. We may see anemic dividend growth for a year or two as Mid-America makes its way through this rough patch, but dividend growth should resume at a higher rate in a couple of years.

Risks

Investors are worried about two main things here.

The first is apartment supply. Apartment starts have slowed significantly in most of MAA's markets, but there are still units that are expected to hit the market in 2026 and 2027. Long-term the situation should be fine, but there could be more weakness in the short-term. Especially if the economy struggles.

The other risk is interest rates. If rates continue to spike, then every apartment REIT will suffer as they renew existing debt or are forced to borrow more. Interest is one of a REIT's biggest costs; any increase there will flow straight to the bottom line. And not in a good way, either.

The Bottom Line

I believe MAA shares are cheap no matter how you slice it. You're buying unloved assets that are working through a very fixable problem. There is a glut of new supply currently on the market, but that will correct itself in a year or two as folks continue migrating to the Sunbelt.

Shares are cheap on a cap rate basis or on a per-unit basis. And they're quite cheap when you compare them to replacement value.

MAA is well-equipped to make it through even the worst downturn unscathed. Residential apartments tend to hold up well during recessions -- after all, everybody needs a place to live -- and it has a fortress balance sheet. It should be able to bring its new development projects online while maintaining that balance sheet strength, too.

I've been adding this one to my portfolio. I rate shares a buy.

data.title: Treasury Yields Snapshot: April 2, 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The yield on the 10-year note finished April 2, 2026, at 4.31%. Meanwhile, the 2-year note ended at 3.79%, and the 30-year yield ended at 4.88%.

The chart below overlays the daily performance of several Treasury bonds, starting from the pre-recession equity market peaks, along with the Federal Funds Rate (FFR) since 2007.

This next table shows the highs and lows of yields and the Federal Funds Rate since 2007.

A Long-Term Look at the 10-Year Treasury Yield

Here is a long-term view of the 10-year yield starting in 1965, well before the 1973 oil embargo that triggered the era of 'stagflation' (economic stagnation coupled with inflation)

Inverted Yield Curve

An inverted yield curve is when longer-term Treasury yields are lower than their shorter-term counterparts. The next chart displays the latest 10-2 spread. Typically, the spread turns negative for a period before rising again prior to recessions, as illustrated in the four recessions shown on this chart. For this reason, the 10-2 spread is widely considered a reliable leading indicator for recessions. The lead time between a negative spread and the onset of a recession varies, with recessions beginning anywhere from 18 to 92 weeks after the spread goes negative.

One false positive is seen in 1998, where the spread briefly went negative without leading to a recession. In the case of the 2009 recession, the spread went negative multiple times before rising again. Most recently, the spread was continuously negative from July 5, 2022, to August 26, 2024. The last time the spread was negative was on September 5, 2024.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date before a recession, the average lead time is 18.5 weeks, or about 4.25 months.

For another perspective on the yield curve, the 10-3mo spread below uses an even shorter-term maturity. The lead time to recessions based on this spread (after it turns negative) ranges from 34 to 69 weeks. Like the 10-2 spread, we see the same false positive in 1998, as well as multiple instances of the spread turning negative before rising again ahead of the 2009 recession. Recently, the spread was negative from October 25, 2022, to December 12, 2024. Since February 26th, the spread has swung back and forth from positive to negative territory.

If we consider the first negative spread date as the starting point, the average lead time to a recession is 48 weeks, or about eleven months. If we instead use the last positive spread date after the spread had been negative, the average lead time is 13 weeks, or about three months.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

The Federal Funds Rate influences the cost of borrowing for banks. When the Fed increases this rate, banks often raise their lending rates, which can impact mortgage rates, among other things. Therefore, a rising FFR generally leads to higher mortgage rates, and vice versa. However, this was not the case recently when the Fed began their rate-cutting cycle in September 2024 and mortgage rates moved in the opposite direction. With that said, mortgage rates have been on the decline as of late, following a similar fashion of the FFR. The latest Freddie Mac Weekly Primary Mortgage Market Survey put the 30-year fixed rate at 6.46%, its highest level since September.

Now let's see the 10-year against the S&P 500 with some notes on Federal Reserve intervention. Fed policy has been a major influence on market behavior.

ETFs associated with Treasuries include: Vanguard 0-3 Month Treasury Bill ETF (VBIL), Vanguard Intermediate-Term Treasury ETF (VGIT), and Vanguard Long-Term Treasury ETF (VGLT).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GNR: A Beneficiary Of Rising Energy Prices
data.description:
In a year when most ETFs have posted noticeable losses from the first quarter, the performance of the State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF (GNR) certainly stands out in the crowd. The fund has gained over 20% in the first three months of 2025. As with most funds of its kind, investors can use GNR to benefit from rising commodity prices, especially the prices of natural resources like oil, agricultural products, and precious metals. With oil prices up over 40% in the last month alone, many of the stocks held by GNR have climbed in response.

How long these recent trends will last is anyone’s guess at this point. An immediate end to the Middle Eastern conflict is not yet in sight, but few expect military action in Iran to extend into the second half of this year. I have written an analysis for two other natural resource ETFs in the last few months. Last December, I wrote about the VanEck Natural Resources ETF (HAP). This January, I added commentary on the American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF (MGNR), noting its heavy allocation toward precious metals and mining stocks.

Each of these funds has a slightly different investment approach within the same general category of natural resources. GNR appears to be in the right place at the right time, currently, but the cyclicality of its holdings, especially in the energy sector, will likely bring it back toward the mean in the months ahead. I consider the fund to be only a Hold, in spite of its strong start to 2026.

ETF Overview

The State Street SPDR S&P Global Nat Resources ETF tracks the results that correspond to the total return performance of the S&P® Global Natural Resources Index. This index is made up of 90 of the largest global companies that are engaged in the businesses related to natural resources and commodities. These 90 companies are selected from three “sub-indices” - the S&P Global Natural Resources - Agriculture Index, the S&P Global Natural Resources - Energy Index and the S&P Global Natural Resources - Metals and Mining Index. Each of these has a weight of one-third of the larger index that is followed by GNR. No stock can have an allocation in the broader index of more than 5%. The index is reconstituted on the last trading day in August and rebalanced in February, May, and November.

As of the close of the market on April 2, GNR has $4.9 billion in assets under management. The top 10 equity holdings in the fund are shown below with a comparison to the allocation found in the ETF's benchmark index. Two stocks—Exxon (XOM) and Shell plc (SHEL)—have weights above 5% because they have increased in value since the last time the ETF’s holdings were rebalanced.

In total, these 10 companies listed comprise 36.3% of the fund’s assets. They include four oil giants, four companies in the mining business, and two involved in the agriculture industry. Company number three on the list, Nutrien Ltd. (NTR) is a name that may be new to many investors. A Canadian provider of chemicals, fertilizer, and agricultural services, NTR has a market cap of $35.9 billion and has gained just over 50% in the last 12 months. Another less well-known name, UPM-Kymmene Oyj (UPMMY), is a Finnish producer of timber and paper products. Its stock has produced a total return of 19.1% over the last year.

As one would expect with almost any natural resources ETF, the sector allocation for GNR is primarily concentrated in basic materials and energy. Materials hold 56.4% of the fund’s assets. Energy stocks have an allocation of 38.2%. Consumer staples have a weight of 4.6%. The fund shows a 0.82% allocation to the real estate sector, which is entirely based on its ownership of one company, Weyerhaeuser (WY), a REIT that is one of the world’s largest owners of timber.

GNR is truly a global ETF, with almost two-thirds of its assets invested outside the US. Almost 20% of the companies owned by the fund are based in Canada. An additional 11.8% are within the borders of the United Kingdom. The list below shows each of the 13 countries that have a representation in GNR of at least 1%, but the total number of nations represented in the portfolio is 21 as of March 31.

The State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF has a current dividend yield of 2.30%. Distributions are made twice each year, with the next payout due to hit investors’ accounts in early June. The $1.72 per share paid out in FY 2025 was 27.1% less than the $2.36 distributed in FY 2024. As shown in the graph below, the fund’s current yield is its lowest since Q2 2021.

The fund’s expense ratio of 0.40% may not sound great to investors who are accustomed to the single-digit fees charged by larger, more traditional US equity ETFs. However, for a global fund that invests in just a few market sectors in over 20 countries, the ratio charged by State Street seems much more reasonable. It is lower than the expenses reported from the two other natural resource ETFs that I have covered in the last five months. The VanEck Natural Resources ETF charges investors 0.42%. The American Beacon GLG Natural Resources ETF has an expense ratio of 0.75%.

Analysis of GNR

The returns from GNR over the last several years have been impressive, regardless of its sector allocations. The State Street website only has the fund's returns updated through the end of February, but as can be seen below, investors in the ETF have been able to enjoy double-digit returns on average for the last decade. Even when tax considerations are made, GNR has been able to produce annual gains of over 10% dating back to early 2017.

The fund’s valuation and growth metrics that are given on Morningstar paint a somewhat mixed picture of the prospects for the fund moving forward. When compared to the average in the natural resources category, GNR’s holdings have lower PE, price-to-book, and price-to-cash flow ratios. However, they also have lower long-term earnings expectations, lower cash flow growth, and negative sales growth.

Value & Growth Measures

GNR

Nat Resources Average

Price/Earnings

15.16

18.78

Price/Book

1.78

Price/Sales

Cash-Flow Growth %

-3.31

When Morningstar ranks the returns of the State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF against other funds in its category, it has not been able to finish in the top 50% of its roughly 120 peers in the last three calendar years. In 2024, it finished in the bottom quartile. This year has been a better one for GNR, however, as the 20% gains mentioned earlier have put the ETF in the top 20% out of an increasingly crowded field of natural resources funds.

Comparison With Other Natural Resource ETFs

It is important for investors to know that there can be a great deal of variance in the approach for selection of natural resource companies. When I am comparing ETFs, I often like to use ETF.com to show greater detail on the sub-sectors that are used for each fund. In this case, I compared GNR to the two natural resource funds that I wrote about on this website previously, MGNR and HAP.

As can be seen in the chart below, the fund from State Street has a much greater allocation to energy stocks like Exxon and Shell. American Beacon's MGNR is much more focused on non-energy minerals, and miners who have benefited from the elevated price of gold and silver over the last year. VanEck’s approach is somewhat of a compromise between the other two.

This difference in stock selection and the added weight in mineral stocks has given MGNR a significant boost in performance over the last two years. The fund has only been trading since February 5, 2024, so its track record is somewhat limited. Nonetheless, the difference in overall return is evident. The graph below starts on the first day that MGNR was traded. Since this date, GNR has had the lowest returns from the group.

Over longer periods of time, HAP has also bested GNR in total returns. Its five-year price performance is over 14% greater. Its 10-year price performance is almost 42% better than that of GNR.

Risks to Consider

Investors should not lose sight of the fact that any investment that tracks the value of natural assets is quite cyclical and involves an elevated amount of risk. Morningstar gives GNR a risk score of 97, which places it right at the top end of its “Very Aggressive” category. The financial services website says that the ETF has been able to capture only 79% of the upside for its benchmark while booking 107% of its downside, not exactly the long-term investment strategy that most would want to follow.

Regulatory changes and a push toward renewable energy in many parts of the world also produce headwinds for many of the holdings in GNR. Many companies are being required to disclose their environmental, social, and governance impacts, which are commonly referred to as ESG standards. Changes in emissions targets and ecological protections can cause shifts in the economics of new projects and business expansion. In the mining industry, the safe storage and monitoring of mining waste, known as “tailings,” holds greater importance to reduce environmental and safety risks. Land rehabilitation and water use restrictions add operational costs. This is a risk not only for the State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF but also for its peer funds mentioned here.

The Bottom Line

Neither GNR nor any of the other natural resource ETFs are intended to be part of a core investment strategy. These investments are cyclical in nature and will likely have periods of outperformance mixed in with periods where they underperform. Things have been good for GNR in 2026 so far, but a resolution to the turmoil in the Middle East could negatively impact the returns from many of the fund's oil producers.

I consider the State Street SPDR S&P Global Natural Resources ETF to be a Hold for those investors who believe that oil and other related assets will continue their climb in the second quarter of this year. However, I still prefer the VanEck Natural Resources ETF as the top option for investors in this space, as it offers the most balanced approach to investing in natural resources.

data.title: Top 25 High-Yield Dividend Stocks For April 2026
data.description:
Market Recap

It's a good thing that March is finally over as it was not a kind month for equities unless you were heavily allocated to the Energy sector. The SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) fell by 4.93% last month and is now down 4.36% year-to-date. The sour performance extended to safer corners of the market as well with the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) declining by 2.62% and slowing down its strong momentum in 2026. Albeit, despite this setback, SCHD is still up 12.79% on the year, and enjoying a very healthy lead over SPY.

Regardless which way the market moves investors shouldn't dwell on what already happened but instead look to the future. As such I ran my monthly stock screening process looking for opportune dividend stocks to consider in the current month.

The main objective for this stock screening process is to have it present me with potentially opportune investment ideas to consider for further review. And whenever the market sees a strong decline as it did in March, I typically find more opportunities. I prefer for these opportunities to also offer a reasonable starting dividend yield and to have the potential to deliver a double digit total return in the long-run.

In past editions of this monthly series I meticulously tracked performance, but over time this became very burdensome. In the past few updates I spent more time retrieving and analyzing individual stock returns than I did putting the list of ideas together. Going forward I will not track and update the performance every month, instead I will repurpose my efforts to find and present more opportunities. Starting this month the Top 10 High Yield list is being expanded to the Top 25 High Yield list. I will still share performance updates but they will be less frequent and compiled in a more efficient manner.

As stated earlier, the primary purpose of this list is to generate investment ideas for further review. My selection process focuses on identifying potentially high-quality companies trading for attractive prices, with an emphasis on a respectable starting dividend yield and a focus on future return potential.

The stock selection process remains unchanged, if you'd like to read about it you can do so in the first article of the series here.

April 2026 Watchlist

Here are the stocks chosen by my methodology. The data shown in the image below is compiled from Charles Schwab and Finviz as of 04/01/26.

At the bottom of the table you'll find the average metrics for the top 25 chosen stocks and the 58 stocks that passed the initial screening process and were included in the final ranking.

The top 25 stocks, on average, sport a 3.86% starting dividend yield, exceeding the average yield of 3.46% for the full dataset. They are overwhelmingly more attractively valued with the average undervaluation being ~34% based on dividend yield theory.

The expected long-term future rate of return "Ex. Ret." is calculated by summing the current dividend yield, the potential upside from a return to fair value over the next 5 years, and a long-term earnings growth forecast. The top 25 stocks have an average potential future CAGR of 19.07% compared to 14.24% for all 58 analyzed stocks.

Please note that my return forecasts are based on assumptions and should be interpreted as rough estimates. However, I do believe the presented companies have the potential to generate better returns over the next five years compared to the other high-yield stocks that passed the initial screening process but were not chosen to be amongst the 25 best ranked stocks.

The 5-year historical dividend yield shown in the image above came directly from Charles Schwab. In the next section, I share my own Dividend Yield Theory valuation model that may present different historical dividend yields and future return potential.

Breaking Down The List

25 stocks may be too many for you to consider during a single calendar month, therefore I have broken down this list into smaller subsets focused on different dividend and investing aspects. If your goal is to lean towards higher staring yields, faster dividend growth or some combination of both, the following shortlists may be more opportune starting points than the Top 25 list shown in the prior section.

High Yield Opportunities

These stocks currently present the most attractive forward dividend yields:

  1. Campbell's Co: 7.00%
  2. United Parcel Service Inc.: 6.67%
  3. General Mills Inc.: 6.56%
  4. Paychex: 4.69%
  5. Comcast: 4.60%

Fast Dividend Growth

These stocks have the best track record of above average dividend growth:

  1. Autoliv Inc: 38.15%
  2. Penske Automotive Group Inc.: 33.37%
  3. SBA Communications Corp: 19.01%
  4. Accenture PLC: 13.66%
  5. Automatic Data Processing: 13.66%

*I skipped over Lear Corp that has a 5 year dividend growth rate of 19.38% because it hasn't increased its dividend in the last few years.

Highest Expected Return

These stocks have the highest future return estimates when excluding the return to fair value component:

  1. Fifth Third Bancorp: 17.64%
  2. Lear Corp: 16.34%
  3. Thomson Reuters Corp: 15.82%
  4. Regions Financial Corp: 14.79%
  5. Autoliv Inc.: 14.71%

Most Undervalued

These are the most potentially undervalued stocks based on Dividend Yield Theory:

  1. Nike: -63.81%
  2. Brown Forman Corporation: -60.52%
  3. SBA Communications: -52.05%
  4. Accenture PLC: -51.94%
  5. Campbell's Co: -51.08%

Fastest Fundamental Growth

These are the stocks with the highest 5-year revenue growth rates:

  1. M&T Bank: 20.12%
  2. Fifth Third Bancorp: 12.19%
  3. Essential Utilities: 11.09%
  4. Regions Financial Corp: 10.66%
  5. Accenture PLC: 9.47%

Best Combination of Dividend Factors

These are the best stocks based on a combination of the dividend yield, dividend growth and payout ratio. The objective here is to find strong balances between all 3 metrics:

  1. Autoliv Inc: 3.31% yield + 38.15% DGR + 32.38% payout ratio
  2. Penske Automotive Group: 3.58% yield + 33.37% DGR +36.75% payout ratio
  3. Harley Davidson: 3.71% yield + 10.35% DGR +25.5% payout ratio
  4. Comcast: 4.60% yield + 7.49% DGR + 24.91% payout ratio
  5. McCormick & Company: 3.81% yield +7.74% DGR +29.89% payout ratio

Custom Dividend Yield Theory Valuation

Let's take a closer look at each of the 25 chosen stocks through my custom dividend yield theory valuation tool. The tool uses daily prices to calculate rolling historical average yields, therefore it can be more precise compared to the average yield reported by Schwab in the original image.

The intention of this section is to further verify the accuracy of the dividend yield theory valuation, review historical dividend growth and break down the components and catalysts for the future return potential.

*Please note that the data shown in the table earlier came from Charles Schwab, while the data shown in the charts below came from Seeking Alpha and Google Finance. Hence, there may be some discrepancies, especially in the historical dividend yield and expected rate of return. The data below is shown as of April 2, 2026 which also created a one trading day discrepancy in the valuation and future return potential.

SBA Communications Corp (SBAC)

SBAC has a short but strong track record of dividend growth. The one caveat is that almost the entire future return estimate is tied to valuation expansion.

Paychex Inc. (PAYX)

PAYX has a strong dividend growth history for a stock that pays a 4.71% forward dividend yield. The company has seen negative pressure from the SaaSpocalypse and presents an interesting valuation opportunity not seen since the pandemic.

Nike Inc. (NKE)

Dividend growth has been cooling off for NKE much as the share price has struggled to gain any momentum since 2022. The stock is currently offering its highest forward dividend yield of the last 7 years but a questionable future with its brand losing value in the market.

Accenture PLC (ACN)

ACN has a very strong dividend growth track record and similar to PAYX it has been impacted by the SaaSpocalypse. The stock currently sports its highest forward dividend yield relative to the last 7 years.

Autoliv Inc. (ALV)

ALV has dipped recently following strong momentum in 2025, once again presenting a more attractive entry point for long term investors. The company cut its dividend during the pandemic but resumed increases shortly thereafter.

Penske Automotive Group Inc. (PAG)

PAG's share price has traded sideways for over 2 years now, deviating from its implied fair value that has risen as a results of continued dividend increases. The gap has currently expanded to roughly 36%, presenting a rather unique opportunity. The company cut its dividend during the pandemic but otherwise has a very strong track record of growth.

Automatic Data Processing (ADP)

Much like PAYX and ACN, ADP is suffering from negative market sentiment surrounding SaaS companies and their ultimate demise at the hands of AI. Whether that comes to fruition is up in the air, but for the time being ADP is once again offering a pandemic high dividend yield above 3%. ADP has a solid track record of strong dividend growth.

Cohen & Steers Inc. (CNS)

CNS's dividend history looks shaky due to special dividends that the company stopped paying in 2021. Removing the special dividends reveals a consistent streak of dividend growth. The stock has only recently dipped below its implied fair value, the first time we have seen this in a few years.

Canadian National Railway Co. (CNI)

Up until 2023, CNI has traded very much in line with its implied fair value. Since early 2024 we have seen the share price dip and diverge from historical dividend multiples. The stock is currently offering a 7-year high forward dividend yield close to 2.6%. The company has a mixed history of dividend growth with some above average increases, however more recently the annual increases have been below average.

Amdocs Ltd (DOX)

Following the most recent sell-off in March, DOX is for the first time in 7 years offering a +3% yield. DOX has a very strong track record of dividend growth with only 2 out of the last 12 years seeing less than double digit increases. For what it's worth, both increase were very close to 10%.

McCormick & Company Inc. (MKC)

MKC has struggled since early 2022 and shares dipped once again last month. This pushed the forward dividend yield very close to 4%, a rarely visited range for the company. Dividend growth has been rather strong especially considering the premium dividend yield at the moment.

Lear Corp (LEA)

LEA has a very mixed track record of dividend growth, first cutting its dividend during the pandemic and then not increasing the dividend for the last few years. The share price has seen strong momentum in the second half of 2025 and is holding up quite well in 2026. The company also has one of the highest projected EPS growth rates of the 25 stock highlighted in this analysis.

PepsiCo Inc. (PEP)

PEP has a solid track record of dividend growth, hovering right around 6% on average, measured over the long term. The price has trended in-line with its implied fair value through 2024, then in 2025 it dipped, more recently we have seen some momentum from the stock but there is still a ~15% gap to where dividend yield theory suggests it should be trading.

Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. (KDP)

KDP's price has struggled since 2022, in turn driving the dividend yield higher as management continued increasing the annual dividend rate. More recent dividend increases have trended lower, and overall the company has a mixed dividend growth track record.

Essential Utilities (WTRG)

WTRG has maintained a healthy dividend growth rate in the 6-7% range during the past decade. Paired with its currently elevated +3% yield makes its a rather attractive utility to consider. It's worth noting my custom dividend yield theory analysis below is showing a future return estimate of 10.76%, below my ideal cutoff of 12%.

Comcast Corp (CMCSA)

After peaking in late 2021, CMCSA's price has struggled to bounce back to its implied fair value. More recently in 2025 and in the past month the price dipped some more, driving the forward dividend yield north of 4%, the first time this has occurred in the last 7 years.

Harley Davidson Inc. (HOG)

HOG has lost more than 50% of its value since early 2023, and the stock has struggled to gain any momentum in the last few years. HOG is one of the few stocks on this list that is projected to see negative earnings growth in the next 3 to 5 years, meaning its entire return assumption is based on the dividend and valuation expansion.

Thomson Reuters Corp (TRI)

TRI has ramped up dividend growth post pandemic, with a few double digit increases recently. The share price was cut in more than half since late 2025 but its struggling to find a bottom in 2026.

Brown Forman Corp (BF.B)

BF.B has lost more nearly 70% of its value in the past few years which has driven its historically ~1% yield north of 3%. The stock is struggling to find its bottom and with a negative EPS growth projection it may still be early to jump on board expecting a valuation recovery.

United Parcel Service Inc. (UPS)

Similar to the last few stocks UPS's share price has been in a decline since early 2022. There was a brief rally towards the end of 2025 and into early 2026 but that momentum faded last month. The stock's forward yield peaked ~8% just a few months ago, and today it still sports a historically high yield of 6.68%. Dividend growth has stalled to a crawl as the company faces operational issues.

Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB)

Unlike the data from Schwab, my custom valuation model suggests FITB is ~7% overvalued right now. Despite this potential overvaluation the stock still presents strong future return potential of ~16%. This assumption is driven by strong projected EPS growth of ~14% paired with a 3.4% forward dividend yield. The company has a solid track record of dividend growth, however, more recently the dividend increases have cooled off to mid single digits.

General Mills Inc. (GIS)

GIS first dipped in mid-2023, declining to its implied fair value. It then traded in the fair value range through late 2024, then once again the price declined through all of 2025 and continued its negative momentum in 2026. Currently the stock is ~45% below its implied fair price and offers a historically high forward dividend yield of 6.5%. GIS has a below average streak of dividend growth that barely outpaces inflation.

M&T Bank Corp (MTB)

My custom valuation model suggests MTB is potentially more overvalued than initially suggested by the data from Schwab. Based on my calculation the stock is also just shy of a 12% future return estimate. It is worth noting that earnings are projected to grow at an attractive double digit pace.

Regions Financial Corp (RF)

RF has a strong track record of dividend growth with a few below average increases during the pandemic and in the prior year. The stock has traded in-line with its fair value range and finds itself in-line with its implied fair price once again after the most recent dip.

Campbell's Co (CPB)

CPB had a rough end to 2024 and an even worse 2025 with the share price declining heavily. The downward spiral has extended into 2026, which in turn has lifted the forward dividend yield above 7%. The company has a rather poor history of dividend growth, not increasing the annual dividend rate in 6 out of the last 10 years. With a negative earnings growth projection it may not be the most opportune time to expect a recovery from the stock just yet.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report, April 5, 2026
data.description:
The market came within a whisker of earning the status of “correction” when the dip-buyers stepped in to save the day. It was a powerful rally in the holiday-shortened week, with the S&P 500 gaining 1.6%. I will unpack what was going on beneath the headline numbers so we can see where money is going and from where it is coming.

S&P 500 Last 4 Weeks

Monday was a down day, but the other three were up days. Tuesday’s big rally (up 2.91% on the day) jump started the recovery rally. As we will see, all indices and all sectors were higher last week, as the dip-buyers lifted (almost) all boats. It’s the “almost” part that I’m most interested in.

Zoom out to 12 months

We finished March with a decline of 5.1% - the first down month since May of last year. Although we didn’t officially enter correction territory, it felt like we did and the impact on certain assets and sectors make the last month look like a correction. You’ll see what I mean as we go through the rest of the charts and tables.

S&P 500 Monthly Returns

S&P 500 Drawdowns

This chart really shows how strong last week’s rally was. Whether it will keep going next week depends on how investors are going to react to geopolitical events and government reports.

A Look At The Bull Run Since 2022

Here is another chart that was calling out for an oversold bounce in the week prior to last week. We were very oversold on a technical basis, so I worry that the bounce was more mechanical than fundamental.

Major Index Performance Last Week

NASDAQ stocks took the lead last week as the Mag 7 had a very strong rally. All of the indices I track were up strongly, but not all of the components of these indices participated in the fun.

Major Asset Class Performance

U.S. REITs were tied for the top performing asset class last week, which is curious to me. I suppose it could just be the case that REITs were oversold and had a technical bounce. I would not call this a durable trend unless we get confirmation over the next few weeks.

Equity Sector Performance

The obvious standout on this chart is the 5% drop in energy sector prices. I can say with a fair amount of confidence that energy had gotten overbought and there was some profit taking going on last week. The Mag 7 helped drive Tech and Comm Services to the top of this list.

Factor Returns

All of the factors I track were up last week, with momentum leading the way higher. Yield was in last place, which doesn’t rhyme with the behavior of REITs, which are a prime source of yield. This bears watching.

Growth vs. Value Returns

Growth beat value last week, as investors went fully “risk-on.”

Commodities Returns

Crude oil was again the best performing commodity, but natural gas took a pounding. It had gotten overbought lately, but a decline of 4.8% in a strong market seems extreme to me.

Non-U.S. Markets

Europe took the top two spots in the international markets last week, while India continues to struggle.

Equity Group Performance

The Mag 7 dominated last week. After lagging all year, they finally caught a decent bid and ran up to close the deficit, or at least head in the right direction. They still have a lot of ground to make up YTD (11.6%) but I think they’ll have a hard time, given the broadening of the market to include smaller companies and energy plays.

The S&P Mag 7+

META Platforms (META) gained a whopping 9.3% last week. Alphabet (GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA) also had large gains. The only loser in the group was Tesla (TSLA), which gave up 0.3%.

The S&P Top 7+ Share of Gains

The S&P 500 is now down 3.8% YTD, and most of that is due to the weakness of the Mag 7 stocks. The other 493 stocks are outperforming the Mag 7 so far. I think this is a durable trend.

The Ten Best-Performing ETFs YTD

Energy-related ETFs dominate the YTD winners this year. One could say that, if it comes from the ground, it’s probably outperforming. The only outlier on this list is Japan, which is up 11.2% YTD.

The Ten Worst-Performing ETFs YTD

Bitcoin replaced Cannabis as the worst performing ETF YTD. In fact, Cannabis dropped 5 spots on the list, due to a strong rebound last week (up 17.4%).

The Ten Best-Performing Momentum Stocks Year-to-Date

Sandisk was back to its winning streak after gaining 9% last week alone. Missing are the other memory storage stocks, Western Digital (WDC) and Micron Tech (MU). Those two might be worth a look.

Final Thoughts

The big story of last week was the pullback in energy (especially natural gas) and the surge in the Mag 7. These could both turn out to be simply counter-trend rallies that reverse once again, so I’ll be watching them closely next week.

To recap the last week’s action, investors were:

  • Buying all the indices
  • Buying all the asset classes
  • Buying all the sectors except energy
  • Buying growth over value
  • Buying momentum names
  • Buying both U.S. stocks and Foreign stocks
